sections.header: Verses 1–12 — "Who Are You to Judge Someone Else’s Servant?"

heb.gloss: to be weak / to lack strength
heb.paragraph: Astheneō describes the ‘weak in faith’ (v. 1) — believers whose conscience is scrupulous about matters Paul considers indifferent (food laws, sacred days). The ‘weakness’ is not moral or spiritual inferiority but an incomplete grasp of gospel freedom. Paul’s sympathy lies with the ‘strong’ (15:1), but his pastoral concern is for the protection of the ‘weak.’

refs.note: Paul’s parallel treatment of the strong/weak dynamic regarding food offered to idols.
Let no one judge you in matters of food, drink, festival, or sabbath — these are shadows of the reality in Christ.
Jesus’ prohibition of judgmental condemnation of others.

notes.note: The ‘weak in faith’ are to be welcomed without quarrelling over disputable matters. The key word is ‘disputable’ (dialogismoi): these are matters on which Scripture does not speak definitively.
The rhetorical question is sharp: who are you to judge someone else’s servant? Each believer is accountable to Christ as Lord, not to the opinions of fellow believers.

Each person must be ‘fully convinced in their own mind.’ Paul affirms the right of individual conscience in matters of indifference while insisting that all must act from a motive of honouring the Lord.
We will all stand before God’s judgment seat. This eschatological perspective relativises human judgment: the only verdict that ultimately matters is God’s.
Calvin identifies the ‘weak’ as those who cling to outward observances from a tender conscience, not from rebellion. They are to be treated with gentleness, not contempt.
Calvin emphasises that the judgment seat of God removes all human pretension to judge another’s standing. Only God knows the heart; only God renders the final verdict.
The phrase dialogismōn (‘opinions’ / ‘disputable matters’) refers to personal judgments or scruples about matters not explicitly commanded or forbidden in Scripture.
The phrase ‘fully convinced’ (plērophoreisthō) denotes a state of settled conviction. Paul requires not unanimity but individual certainty before God.

Moo argues that Paul’s principle is broader than the specific issues of food and days: wherever Christians disagree on matters of adiaphora (things indifferent), the governing principle is mutual acceptance and accountability to Christ as Lord.

Schreiner emphasises that Paul’s resolution is theocentric: Christ is Lord of both the living and the dead (v. 9), and every knee will bow to God (v. 11). This lordship of Christ over all believers makes mutual judgment inappropriate.

hist.context: Romans 14:1–15:13 addresses a concrete pastoral problem in the Roman house churches: conflict between ‘weak’ and ‘strong’ believers over food and sacred days. The ‘weak’ likely included Jewish Christians who continued to observe dietary laws and festival days, while the ‘strong’ were predominantly Gentile Christians who considered such observances unnecessary. Paul’s resolution is remarkable: neither group should judge the other (v. 4), because each person stands before Christ as Lord. The principle of mutual acceptance for the sake of unity is the governing concern.

sections.header: Verses 13–23 — "Do Not Cause Your Brother or Sister to Stumble"

heb.paragraph: Proskomma (v. 13) denotes an obstacle placed in someone’s path that causes them to trip. Paul applies it to any action by the ‘strong’ that causes the ‘weak’ to act against their conscience. The principle is that freedom must be limited by love: the strong believer voluntarily restricts their liberty to avoid damaging a weaker brother or sister.

refs.note: Paul warns that the exercise of freedom can become a stumbling block to the weak — the same principle applied to idol-meat.
Jesus warns about causing ‘little ones’ to stumble — the theological foundation for Paul’s concern.
Whether you eat or drink, do everything for the glory of God — the overriding principle.

notes.note: Stop judging and start building. Paul redirects energy from criticism to constructive love.
Paul affirms that no food is intrinsically unclean. This is a dramatic statement from a former Pharisee and reflects the teaching of Jesus (Mark 7:19).
The kingdom of God is not about eating and drinking. Paul elevates the discussion from peripheral disputes to the kingdom’s core: righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit.

Whatever does not proceed from faith is sin. This is the principle of conscience: acting against personal conviction violates one’s own integrity before God, regardless of the action’s objective morality.

Calvin agrees that no food is unclean in itself but insists that it becomes unclean to anyone who considers it unclean. Subjective conviction creates real moral obligation, even when objectively mistaken.
Calvin calls this the ‘golden rule of Christian liberty’: freedom in externals, unity in essentials, love in all things.
The word koinos (‘common’ / ‘unclean’) in this context reflects Jewish ceremonial categories. Paul uses the word Jesus effectively abolished (Mark 7:19): no food is inherently defiling.

The principle that ‘everything that does not come from faith is sin’ applies specifically to the conscience of the weak believer. It is not a universal principle about uncertainty but about acting against conviction.

Moo argues that Paul’s ethic here demonstrates the ‘other-directed’ character of Christian freedom. Freedom is real but must be exercised in the service of love. The strong are called to bear the burden of self-limitation for the sake of the weak.

Schreiner reads v. 17 as Paul’s definition of kingdom priorities. The triad of righteousness, peace, and joy displaces the triad of food, drink, and days. The kingdom is spiritual and eschatological, not ceremonial.

Schreiner emphasises that conscience, while fallible, is binding: one may not act against conscience without sin, even when conscience is objectively wrong. This creates the pastoral obligation to educate conscience rather than override it.

hist.context: Romans 14:13–23 shifts from the prohibition of judging (vv. 1–12) to the limitation of freedom (vv. 13–23). Paul agrees with the ‘strong’ theologically (‘I am convinced that nothing is unclean in itself,’ v. 14) but sides with the ‘weak’ pastorally. The governing principle is that the kingdom of God is not about food and drink but about righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit (v. 17). This verse is one of Paul’s most concise definitions of the kingdom’s essence.

rows.label: vv.1–4
rows.text: Accept the Weak: Do Not Judge

rows.label: vv.5–12
rows.text: Each Is Accountable to the Lord

rows.label: vv.13–19
rows.text: Freedom Limited by Love: The Kingdom’s Priorities

lit.note: Romans 14 addresses the pastoral crisis of the strong and weak in the Roman house churches. Paul’s twin principles: do not judge (vv. 1–12) and do not cause stumbling (vv. 13–23).

themes.note: Judgment and Mercy both score 7: the chapter balances divine judgment (before which all stand) and the call to merciful forbearance toward brothers and sisters. Faith at 7 reflects the conscience-and-conviction theme.

hebtext.note: Astheneō describes the ‘weak in faith’ (v. 1) — believers whose conscience is scrupulous about matters Paul considers indifferent (food laws, sacred days). The ‘weakness’ is not moral or spiritual inferiority but an incomplete grasp of gospel freedom. Paul’s sympathy lies with the ‘strong’ (15:1), but his pastoral concern is for the protection of the ‘weak.’

Proskomma (v. 13) denotes an obstacle placed in someone’s path that causes them to trip. Paul applies it to any action by the ‘strong’ that causes the ‘weak’ to act against their conscience. The principle is that freedom must be limited by love: the strong believer voluntarily restricts their liberty to avoid damaging a weaker brother or sister.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: The ‘weak’ likely included Jewish Christians who continued to observe dietary laws and festival days, while the ‘strong’ were predominantly Gentile Christians who considered such observances unnecessary…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Weak and the Strong

translations.text: Whoever regards one day as special does so to the Lord. Whoever eats meat does so to the Lord, for they give thanks to God; and whoever abstains does so to the Lord and gives thanks to God.

The one who observes the day, observes it in honor of the Lord. The one who eats, eats in honor of the Lord, since he gives thanks to God, while the one who abstains, abstains in honor of the Lord and gives thanks to God.
The one who eats everything must not treat with contempt the one who does not, and the one who does not eat everything must not judge the one who does, for God has accepted them.
Let not the one who eats despise the one who abstains, and let not the one who abstains pass judgment on the one who eats, for God has welcomed him.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "The ‘weak in faith’ are to be welcomed without quarrelling over disputable matters. The key word is ‘disputable’ (dialogismoi): these are matters on which Scripture does not speak definitively."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin identifies the ‘weak’ as those who cling to outward observances from a tender conscience, not from rebellion. They are to be treated with gentleness, not contempt."

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The phrase dialogismōn (‘opinions’ / ‘disputable matters’) refers to personal judgments or scruples about matters not explicitly commanded or forbidden in Scripture."

thread.text: Paul’s parallel treatment of the strong/weak dynamic regarding food offered to idols.
Let no one judge you in matters of food, drink, festival, or sabbath — these are shadows of the reality in Christ.
Jesus’ prohibition of judgmental condemnation of others.
Paul warns that the exercise of freedom can become a stumbling block to the weak — the same principle applied to idol-meat.
Jesus warns about causing ‘little ones’ to stumble — the theological foundation for Paul’s concern.
Whether you eat or drink, do everything for the glory of God — the overriding principle.

discourse.thesis: Accept the one weak in faith; do not quarrel over opinions or pass judgment — each person is accountable to the Lord.

nodes.text: As for the one weak in faith, welcome him — but not to quarrel over opinions. One person believes he may eat anything; another eats only vegetables. Let not the one who eats despise the one who abstains, and let not the abstainer judge the one who eats, for God has welcomed him. Who are you to pass judgment on the servant of another? It is before his own master that he stands or falls.

children.text: And he will be upheld, for the Lord is able to make him stand.

nodes.text: One person esteems one day as better than another; another esteems all days alike. Let each be fully convinced in his own mind. The one who observes the day observes it in honor of the Lord; the one who eats eats in honor of the Lord, since he gives thanks.
None of us lives to himself, and none dies to himself. Whether we live or die, we are the Lord's. Christ died and lived again to be Lord of both the dead and the living.

Why do you pass judgment on your brother? Or why do you despise your brother? We will all stand before the judgment seat of God. As it is written, 'Every knee shall bow to me, and every tongue shall confess to God.' Each of us will give an account of himself to God.

Let us not pass judgment on one another any longer, but resolve never to put a stumbling block or hindrance in a brother's way. Nothing is unclean in itself — but it is unclean for anyone who thinks it unclean. If your brother is grieved by what you eat, you are no longer walking in love. Do not destroy by your food one for whom Christ died. Do not let your good be spoken of as evil. The kingdom of God is not a matter of eating and drinking but of righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit.

children.text: The one who serves Christ in these things is acceptable to God and approved by men.

nodes.text: Pursue peace and mutual upbuilding. Do not destroy the work of God for the sake of food. Everything is clean, but it is wrong to eat anything that causes another to stumble. It is good not to eat meat or drink wine or do anything that causes your brother to stumble. Keep your faith between yourself and God. Blessed is the one who has no reason to pass judgment on himself for what he approves. Whoever has doubts is condemned if he eats, because eating is not from faith. Whatever does not proceed from faith is sin.

discourse.note: Romans 14 addresses disputes over food laws and holy days in the mixed Jewish-Gentile Roman church. Paul's principle: freedom of conscience is limited by love for the weaker brother. The 'judgment seat of God' (βῆμα τοῦ θεοῦ) shifts evaluation from horizontal (brother-to-brother) to vertical (each before God).

coaching.tip: 'As for the one who is weak in faith, welcome him, but not to quarrel over opinions' (v. 1). Disputable matters — diet, days — don't warrant division. The 'weak' have scruples; the 'strong' have freedom. Both answer to the Lord. Neither should despise or judge the other. Unity transcends uniformity.

'We will all stand before the judgment seat of God' (v. 10). Personal accountability to God relativizes judgments between believers. 'Why do you pass judgment on your brother?' (v. 10). Let God sort the disputable; focus on your own standing. Judgment-seat awareness promotes humility, not harshness.
